content.text: An Algorithmic Impact Assessment produces value only when its findings drive tangible risk reduction. The bridge between an AIA finding and a measurably safer system is the impact mitigation plan — a structured commitment that names a specific control, assigns clear ownership, sets a deadline, and records evidence of completion. This sub-subtopic teaches you how to design those plans, how to run the tracking process that keeps them on schedule, and how to close them out in a way that satisfies regulators and auditors.

attrs.alt: Impact Mitigation Plan Lifecycle — four phases: AIA Finding, Mitigation Plan, Tracking, and Verification & Closure

content.text: Why mitigation plans fail without a tracking system

Impact assessments that generate findings but no tracked remediation create a compliance liability greater than having done no assessment at all — they document awareness without action. The three most common failure modes are:
Diffuse ownership
— a finding is assigned to a team rather than a named individual, so no one acts until a review surfaces the gap months later.
Undated commitments
— controls are described but have no deadline, making them impossible to track against a schedule or use in audit evidence.
Missing residual risk estimates
— the plan states what will be done but not what risk level is expected to remain afterward, preventing meaningful verification.
Structure of an impact mitigation plan
Each plan record corresponds to one finding from the AIA and contains the following mandatory fields.
Field
Type
Purpose
finding_id
string
Links plan back to the AIA finding record
control_description
string
Concrete action to be taken (not a category label)
control_type
enum
technical | procedural | contractual | training
owner
string
Named individual (role + name), not a team
due_date
date
Absolute date, not a relative offset
target_residual_score
int
Expected risk score after control is in place
status
enum
open | in_progress | done | overdue | accepted
evidence_refs
list[str]
Links to test reports, PR numbers, config commits
Python data model
The following model captures the full lifecycle of a mitigation plan record, including status transitions and closure validation.
The four phases in practice
Phase 1 — Translating AIA findings into plan records
An AIA finding states what is wrong and how bad it is. The mitigation plan must state what will be done about it. The translation step requires:

Converting the finding's risk category into a specific control rather than a generic safeguard class. Writing 'implement output filtering' is insufficient; writing 'deploy regex blocklist v2.1 on the loan-decision prompt channel before 2026-09-01' is actionable.
Estimating the target residual score by modelling how much the proposed control reduces either the severity or the likelihood dimension of the risk score.
Getting sign-off from the named owner before entering the record as 'open', so the owner has acknowledged accountability rather than being assigned without notice.
Phase 2 — Designing controls that actually reduce risk
Controls fall into four types, each appropriate for different risk sources:
Technical
— guardrails embedded in the agent pipeline (output filters, rate limiters, scope-restricted tool grants, human-approval gates before high-stakes actions).
Procedural
— operational rules enforced by people (mandatory review queues, change-management gates, incident escalation checklists). Effective only when combined with monitoring to confirm adherence.
Contractual
— obligations pushed upstream to model providers or downstream to business users via data processing agreements, terms of service, or SLAs.
Training

— staff education to ensure human actors who interact with the agent understand its limitations and approval responsibilities. Lowest residual-risk reduction on its own; effective as a complement to technical controls.
Phase 3 — Running the tracking cadence
Mitigation registers require a structured review cadence, not ad-hoc checking. A recommended schedule:
30-day check-in
— owner confirms work has begun and identifies blockers. Status advances from 'open' to 'in_progress'.
60-day check-in
— owner provides a progress update. Any plans at risk of missing their deadline are flagged for escalation to the AI Governance Lead.
Due date
— owner submits evidence and requests verification. Plans past their due date without evidence automatically flip to 'overdue' and trigger an escalation notification.
90-day hard stop
— any plan still overdue escalates to the risk committee with a mandatory disposition decision: extend deadline (with written rationale), accept residual risk, or suspend the agent.
Evidence collection and verification
A plan cannot be closed without concrete evidence that the control is operating as intended. Evidence requirements by control type:
Technical controls
— automated test results showing the guardrail blocks or flags the target behaviour; a commit or deployment record showing the change is live in production.
Procedural controls
— audit log excerpts confirming the procedure was followed across at least three production cycles; a spot-check result from an independent reviewer.
Contractual controls
— executed agreement version and date, confirmation of counterparty signature.
Training controls
— attendance records or completion logs for all personnel in scope, plus a post-training assessment pass rate.

After evidence review, the verifier re-scores the risk using the same AIA rubric and compares the actual residual score to the target. If the target is met, the plan is closed. If not, a new plan entry is created — the original is not re-opened, to preserve the audit trail.
Risk acceptance as a formal decision

Not every finding can be fully remediated — some risks are inherent to the agent's function, or the cost of full mitigation outweighs the benefit. When this is the case, the organisation must formally accept the residual risk rather than leaving the plan open indefinitely. A risk acceptance record must include:
The accepted residual risk score and the rationale for why it is tolerable.

The level of authority granting acceptance (for scores above 12, the CISO or CPO must sign off; for scores above 14, board-level sign-off is typically required under EU AI Act high-risk classification).
A review date — risk acceptance is time-limited and must be re-evaluated at each AIA revision cycle.
Tooling and integration
Mitigation registers should not live in spreadsheets for systems beyond a prototype stage. At scale, the register integrates with:
GRC platforms
(ServiceNow GRC, OneTrust, Vanta) — link each plan to the AIA finding record, automate due-date alerts, and route evidence to a verifier queue.
CI/CD pipelines
— for technical controls, the commit hash or deployment ID is automatically captured as an evidence reference when the guardrail ships to production.
Model cards and system cards
— the 'Known Limitations' and 'Risks and Mitigations' sections of each card should reference open plan IDs so external reviewers can trace the mitigation status.
Regulatory mapping
Mitigation plans for high-risk AI systems are not optional bookkeeping — they feed directly into regulatory obligations:
EU AI Act Article 9
— requires providers to put in place risk management measures and document their effectiveness throughout the lifecycle. The mitigation register is the primary artefact for this obligation.
NIST AI RMF Map function

— the Manage function requires organisations to prioritise and implement risk response plans. Mitigation records are the primary evidence for the 'MANAGE 1.3 responses to identified risks are communicated' subcategory.
ISO 42001 Clause 8.4
— requires treatment of AI risks with documented treatment options and evidence of implementation. The mitigation register maps directly to this clause.
Sample YAML register entry
A machine-readable register entry suitable for YAML-based GRC tooling or version-controlled policy repositories:

# mitigation-register-v2.yaml
agent_id: loan-decision-assistant-v3
aia_version: "2026-Q1"
plans:
  - finding_id: AIA-2026-Q1-F04
    title: "Disparate approval rate — protected attribute correlation"
    control_description: >
      Deploy post-processing fairness calibration layer (equalised odds)
      on loan approval scores before transmitting to underwriting UI.
      Threshold: demographic parity gap < 3 percentage points per cohort.
    control_type: technical
    owner: "ML Fairness Engineer — P. Adeyemi"
    due_date: "2026-09-15"
    initial_risk_score: 12
    target_residual_score: 6
    status: in_progress
    evidence_refs:
      - "https://github.com/org/loan-ai/pull/884  # calibration PR"
    notes: "30-day check-in 2026-07-28 — implementation 60% complete."

  - finding_id: AIA-2026-Q1-F07
    title: "Insufficient operator override documentation"
    control_description: >
      Update operator runbook to include mandatory documentation step
      whenever agent recommendation is overridden. Integrate with
      ServiceNow ticket flow so overrides are captured automatically.
    control_type: procedural
    owner: "Ops Lead — M. Brennan"
    due_date: "2026-08-01"
    initial_risk_score: 9
    target_residual_score: 4
    status: done
    evidence_refs:
      - "SNOW-2209341  # runbook update ticket"
      - "audit-log-excerpt-2026-07-10.pdf"
    actual_residual_score: 4
    closed_date: "2026-07-10"
Closure and AIA update
Closing a mitigation plan is not the end of the process — it triggers two downstream actions:

The AIA document is updated with the actual residual risk score and the evidence reference. The AIA version number increments. This ensures the AIA always reflects the current state of risk, not the state at the time of the original assessment.

The system card and model card 'Risks and Mitigations' sections are regenerated to reflect the updated risk posture. Any external consumers of these cards (regulators, audit firms, partner organisations) who have pinned a version are notified that a new version is available.
Common mistakes
Closing plans on implementation, not verification
— deploying a guardrail and closing the plan are not the same step. The plan closes when the control is verified to work in production conditions, not when the feature ships.
Conflating severity reduction with likelihood reduction

— a training control primarily reduces likelihood (humans are less likely to misuse the system), while an output filter reduces severity (even if triggered, harm is bounded). Mixing up the target dimension leads to unrealistic residual score estimates.
One plan per finding, one finding per plan

— a single control that addresses five findings should still be modelled as five separate plan records, each linked to the control. This keeps the audit trail unambiguous and allows per-finding closure at different dates.
Key takeaways
A mitigation plan is valid only when it names a specific control, a specific person, a specific deadline, and a specific target residual score.
The tracking cadence (30/60/90 days with automated escalation) prevents plans from silently ageing past their deadline without decision.
Plans close on verified evidence, not on implementation. The verifier re-scores the risk and compares to the target before closure.
Risk acceptance is a formal decision with named authority levels, written rationale, and a mandatory review date — it is not a way to informally close an inconvenient finding.
The mitigation register feeds directly into EU AI Act Article 9, NIST AI RMF Manage function, and ISO 42001 Clause 8.4 obligations.
